checks.a_analysis: The output contains AI-generated section titles like '021722EDUCommittee Agenda' and 'Sept 23 2021 EDU Committee Minutes Draft', which are acceptable for accessibility. The body text and list items accurately reflect the source document without inventing content.
There is no contact information like phone numbers or email addresses in the source document other than the names of attendees. The output does not contain any fabricated contact details.

The JSON output explicitly omits several pages of the document, specifically slides 2 through 10 of the presentation (pages 6, 7, 9, 10, 11, 12, 13, 14, and 15), as indicated by the placeholder '9 sections omitted for token budget'. These pages contain significant data regarding campaign metrics, analytics, and lists of URLs.
checks.b_evidence_source: The source PDF contains presentation slides on pages 6, 7, 9, 10, 11, 12, 13, 14, and 15 covering campaign overview, metrics, and website analytics.
checks.b_evidence_location: Between Section 3 and Section 5 of the JSON output

checks.a_analysis: The attendance tables on page 2 of the minutes are severely truncated. The 'Committee Members' table in the source has 10 data rows, while the output only includes 1. The 'Staff' table in the source has 11 data rows, while the output only includes 1. This represents a loss of over 90% of the data in these tables.
checks.b_evidence_source: The 'Committee Members' table on page 2 lists 10 members (Jeryl Anderson through Donna Wright). The 'Staff' table on page 2 lists 11 members (Richard Bradford through Angie Turbeville).
checks.b_evidence_output: The 'Committee Members and Guests Attendance' table contains only 1 member row (Jeryl Anderson). The 'Staff Attendance' table contains only 1 staff row (Richard Bradford).

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: While there are massive omissions of entire pages (C3) and table rows (C4), the text within the sections that are actually present (such as the agenda and minutes text blocks) is mostly intact.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: The text preservation in the included sections is high. Numbers, dates, and names match the source material correctly across the agenda and the minutes.

checks.a_analysis: The content appears to be unique and matches the structure of the source document. No duplicated blocks of text were found during the analysis of the JSON output.
The heading levels in the JSON use a flat H2 structure for major section titles and slide titles. This is a common and acceptable normalization for accessibility as per the task instructions.

There is a major structural failure in the attendance tables on page 2 of the source document. The 'Committee Members and Guests Attendance' table and the 'Staff Attendance' table in the JSON only contain a single data row each (Jeryl Anderson and Richard Bradford respectively), whereas the source document lists over 10 individuals in each category. This results in significant loss of structural data.
checks.b_evidence_source: The table on Page 2 lists 10 committee members (Anderson, Cole, Greene, Gusa, Hartman, Joyner, Downs, Short, Stewart, Wright) and 11 staff members.
checks.b_evidence_output: The tables in the JSON output only contain one data row for 'Jeryl Anderson' and one for 'Richard Bradford'.
checks.b_evidence_location: Sept 23 2021 EDU Committee Minutes Draft section, page 2

checks.a_analysis: The reading order generally follows the source. Although the 'Next Meeting' text on page 1 was moved from the bottom of the page to the top header section in the JSON, this is an acceptable reordering for grouping related administrative information at the start of the agenda.
Lists, particularly the agenda items in the minutes and the ROI slide, are correctly identified as ordered and unordered lists with appropriate nesting.

checks.check_name: Heading count mismatch between JSON and HTML
checks.a_analysis: The provided HTML metadata indicates a significant discrepancy between the number of headings identified in the semantic JSON (6) and those rendered in the HTML (23). A difference of 17 headings far exceeds the allowed threshold of 10% or 3 headings.
The JSON fails to capture any links (metadata count is 0), even though page 13 of the source document explicitly lists 10 government URLs which should be semantically tagged as links.

checks.a_analysis: The HTML output maintains the visual organization of the source PDF. The multi-page document is converted into a continuous single-column layout which is standard for HTML. Key sections like the agenda, meeting minutes, and presentation slides are clearly delineated and appear in the same relative order as the source. The addition of a 'Contents' sidebar and the movement of the 'Next Meeting' text to the top of its section are acceptable layout adaptations for a web-based document.

Tables throughout the document, including the complex 'PSAP Impacts – Application Intake' table on page 16 and the attendance and roll-call tables, are rendered correctly in the HTML. They retain their structure, headers are distinct, and all cell data is clearly readable and properly aligned.

Several meaningful images from the source presentation slides are missing in the HTML rendering, replaced by solid green rectangular placeholders. Specifically, the analytics dashboard screenshots on PDF pages 10, 11, and 13 are not rendered, depriving the user of significant visual data intended by the original document.
checks.b_evidence_source: Dashboard images on pages 10 (Site Summary), 11 (Engagement), and 13 (Top Clicks) showing Google Analytics data.
checks.b_evidence_output: Solid green rectangular blocks appear under the corresponding headings 'Webpage Analytics: Site Summary', 'Webpage Analytics: Engagement', and 'Webpage Analytics: Top 10 Clicks...'.
checks.b_evidence_location: Sections 'Webpage Analytics: Site Summary', 'Webpage Analytics: Engagement', and 'Webpage Analytics: Top 10 Clicks after accessing the Recruitment Webpage'

checks.a_analysis: A thorough review of the HTML screenshot shows no instances of content blocks (paragraphs, tables, or headers) being repeated or duplicated in error.

Aside from the broken images identified in V3, the overall visual quality is good. The text is legible, spacing is consistent, and the document structure is well-preserved. The broken image placeholders are a major functional defect, but the rest of the document's polish is acceptable.
